answer:
**The 'paimaana' (also transliterated as 'paimana'), meaning a measuring cup or goblet, functions as a recurrent trope in classical Urdu poetry, particularly in the Persianate ghazal tradition represented in this corpus by Mirza Ghalib and Allama Iqbal. It symbolizes a vessel containing wine (*mai* or *sehba*), evoking themes of ecstatic intoxication, spiritual thirst, divine love, worldly illusion, or existential measure—often contrasting finite human capacity with infinite longing.[^1][^2][^3]**

**This imagery draws from Sufi-influenced wine poetry (*mey-khana* motifs), where the paimaana is not merely literal but a metaphor for the portion of wisdom, love, or annihilation (*fana*) allotted to the soul. For instance, Ghalib likens it to a "line" or measure inscribed on glass (*moo-e-sheesha ko samajhte hain khat-e-paimana*), portraying the free spirit as perceiving cosmic scale in minutiae.[^4] Iqbal extends it cosmically, as a cup "for the whole world" (*Ek paimaana tera saare zamaane ke liye*) or brimming with restless tears (*Ashk-e-betaab se labrez hai paimaana tera*), blending personal desire with universal redemption.[^5][^6]**

**The paimaana relates intimately to the *saqi* (cupbearer), who pours or offers it, embodying the beloved, divine intermediary, or agent of grace. The saqi animates the trope: they fill the cup, taunt the drinker with delay or refusal, and catalyze rapture or ruin. Ghalib invokes the saqi directly while evoking the cup's measure (*ba-qadr-e-zarf hai saqi khumar-e-tishna-kaami*), tying thirst's stupor to capacity.[^7] He demands a *paimana-e-sehba* (cup of wine) amid life's childish illusions, underscoring the saqi's role in granting (or withholding) transcendence.[^8] Iqbal critiques modern *saqi*-wine dynamics in nationalist terms (*Is daur mein may aur hai, jaam aur hai / Jam aur Saqi ne bana ki ravish*), implying shifted measures of grace.[^9] Zauq pleads with the saqi for wine (*sharaab*) to quench heart-fire, completing the ritual.[^10]**

**Across these works, the duo forms a dyad: the saqi as giver, paimaana as gift—mirroring lover-beloved, seeker-God dynamics. No explicit treatise defines the trope here (the corpus prioritizes primary verse over criticism), but its pattern illustrates Urdu poetry's mystical hedonism.[^11]**

[^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 51, [p-c3cb04](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/51-gham-nahin-hota-hai-aazaadon-ko-besh-az-yak-nafas/translation/#p-c3cb04).
[^2]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 213, [p-22c599](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/213-baazeecha-e-atfaal-hai-duniya-mere-aage/translation/#p-22c599).
[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4, [p-c80d32](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/transliteration/#p-c80d32).
[^4]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 51, [full chapter](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/51-gham-nahin-hota-hai-aazaadon-ko-besh-az-yak-nafas/translation/).
[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 8, full snippet context.
[^6]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 18, [p-ca079f](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/18-dil/transliteration/#p-ca079f).
[^7]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 231, [p-7a008b](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/231-sarapa-rehn-e-ishq-o-na-guzir-e-ulfat-e-hasti/translation/#p-a37ab4).
[^8]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 213, [full chapter](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/213-baazeecha-e-atfaal-hai-duniya-mere-aage/translation/).
[^9]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 25, [p-a673c0](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/transliteration/#p-a673c0).
[^10]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 58, [p-16e1a7](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/58-kahan-talak-kahoon-saqi-ke-laa-sharaab-to-de/transliteration/#p-16e1a7).
[^11]: Corpus-wide searches yield ~10 paimaana/paimana hits and ~20 saqi, concentrated in these works; no secondary analysis present.
